skill.name: ai-image-generation
skill.description: Generate and edit images through the inference.sh CLI using hosted models such as FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Seedream, Reve, and Topaz.
skill.summary: Create, edit, stitch, and upscale AI images with inference.sh image models.
skill.author: tul-sh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many Ruby backtick execution hits and one weak cryptography hit, but these are false positives from Markdown code formatting and the inference.sh name in SKILL.md. The confirmed risk is that the skill intentionally runs the external infsh CLI and sends prompts or image URLs to hosted image-generation services. No prompt injection attempt or malicious exfiltration logic was found in the reviewed file.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution for Image Services
medium_findings.description: The skill allows and documents Bash execution of infsh commands. This is the intended function, but it can run external model jobs and should be disclosed to users.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowlist and examples clearly invoke the infsh CLI. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but it is still external command execution.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Network Processing of Prompts and Images
medium_findings.description: The skill uses inference.sh-hosted apps and examples that pass prompts or image URLs to remote services. This is expected for image generation, but users should avoid sensitive prompts and private image URLs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation points to inference.sh and shows remote image URL inputs. No secret collection is visible, but network processing is part of the workflow.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static Ruby backtick findings occur in Markdown inline code, fenced bash examples, and model identifiers. They are not Ruby code paths in this skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown and the flagged syntax is documentation formatting. There is no Ruby source file or Ruby execution context.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Finding Is Not Supported
low_findings.description: The static weak crypto finding points to the skill description. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing operation, or encryption code is present there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is descriptive metadata for image-generation features. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or similar weak cryptographic use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Allowlist for infsh Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access for infsh commands. This is scoped to the intended CLI, but it still executes local commands that can contact external services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration explicitly permits Bash(infsh *). The risk is clear, although the scope is limited to the named CLI.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Image URL Inputs
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include image_url and images fields that reference HTTPS resources. These inputs may reveal private image locations to remote inference services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples use placeholder URLs rather than real secrets. The privacy risk depends on the URLs users provide at runtime.

content.user_title: Generate AI Images with Hosted Models
content.value_statement: Creating production-ready visuals often requires testing several image models and settings. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code run inference.sh image apps for generation, editing, stitching, and upscaling.
actual_capabilities: Runs inference.sh image apps through documented infsh CLI commands.
Generates images from text prompts with FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Seedream, Reve, and related models.
Provides examples for image upscaling, image stitching, and text rendering tasks.
Lists model app IDs and their intended use cases for faster model selection.
Shows how to browse available image apps from the CLI.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI to be installed and authenticated before use.
Depends on external inference.sh services and their model availability, pricing, and latency.
Generated results vary by model, prompt quality, parameters, and provider behavior.
Users should not send confidential prompts or private image URLs without approval.

use_cases.title: Product Mockup Creation
use_cases.description: Generate polished product photos or concept images for design reviews and marketing drafts.

use_cases.title: Campaign Visual Exploration
use_cases.description: Compare multiple models and aspect ratios for social posts, ads, banners, and launch assets.

use_cases.title: Image Enhancement Workflow
use_cases.description: Upscale, stitch, or revise generated images before handing them to a design pipeline.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an image of a modern desk setup with natural light. Use a reliable default image model and a square aspect ratio.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Model Outputs
prompt_templates.prompt: Create the same product mockup with FLUX and Gemini. Summarize the visual differences and recommend the stronger result.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Marketing Variants
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate three versions of a launch image for a productivity app: square, widescreen, and vertical. Keep the visual style consistent.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Image Refinement Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a hero image, choose the best result, upscale it, and create a stitched comparison sheet for review.

output_examples.input: A request for a studio product photo of a ceramic mug.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects a suitable image model, runs the generation command, and returns the generated image result with key settings.

output_examples.input: A request to improve a low-resolution generated concept image.
output_examples.output: The assistant uses the upscaling workflow and reports the enhanced image output location and model used.

output_examples.input: A request to create a comparison board from two image URLs.
output_examples.output: The assistant stitches the images horizontally and returns a review-ready comparison image.

best_practices: Start with a clear prompt that names the subject, style, lighting, composition, and aspect ratio.
Use non-sensitive prompts and approved image URLs because requests are processed by external services.
Generate small test batches before using high-resolution or premium models for final assets.
anti_patterns: Do not send confidential product plans, private likenesses, or restricted image URLs without permission.
Do not assume every listed model has the same parameters, output quality, or text rendering behavior.
Do not skip review of generated images before publishing them in customer-facing material.

faq.question: Does this skill generate images directly inside the assistant?
faq.answer: No. It guides the assistant to use the inference.sh CLI to run hosted image-generation apps.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Do I need an inference.sh account?
faq.answer: Yes. The quick start shows an infsh login step, so authentication is expected.

faq.question: Can it edit or upscale existing images?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes examples for upscaling, stitching, and image workflows that use remote image URLs.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential images?
faq.answer: Use caution. The workflow sends prompts or image URLs to external services, so sensitive content needs prior approval.

faq.question: Why is the security risk marked medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it intentionally runs an external CLI and depends on network-based model providers.
